title: Fitting a picture rail
subtitle: Fix a picture rail at cornice level for hanging pictures without wall fixings
excerpt: How to fit a picture rail: finding the correct height below a cornice, marking a level line around the room, cutting and fixing the rail, and finishing for painting. Includes how to use picture hooks on the rail for flexible hanging.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Newey and Drage, Practical Carpentry and Joinery.
aliases: how to fit a picture rail

glossaryTerms.definition: A horizontal wooden moulding fixed to the wall near ceiling level, with a hooked profile on the upper face. Picture hooks slide along the rail, allowing pictures to be hung and repositioned without making new holes in the wall.

content.text: allows pictures and frames to be hung and moved without drilling into the wall. Standard height is 300 mm below the ceiling or 50 to 75 mm below a plaster cornice. Period houses in the UK were built with picture rails as standard; restoring one follows the original fixing lines, which are sometimes still visible as a row of old nail holes.

Measure 300 mm down from the ceiling at several points around the room and mark with a pencil. Join the marks with a horizontal line using a spirit level. Where a cornice is present, work from the lower edge of the cornice rather than the ceiling: position the rail so its top edge sits 50 mm below the cornice, or to match any original fixing marks visible in the plaster.
Step 2, cut and fit the rail

Measure each wall length and cut the rail with a fine-tooth tenon saw. At internal corners, the two pieces of rail can meet with a simple square-cut butt joint (the joined ends are barely visible at picture-rail height). At external corners, cut both pieces at 45 degrees for a mitre joint. Offer each length up to the pencil line and check for level.

Apply grab adhesive to the back of the rail, then nail through the face at 600 mm intervals using cut-floor brads (2.5 mm by 50 mm). Punch the nail heads below the surface. The fixing must be into the plaster and ideally into the masonry or timber ground behind it. On a plasterboard wall, locate the studs and fix to them as a priority; supplement with grab adhesive in the spans between studs.

Fill nail holes with a fine interior filler and sand flush. Fill any gap between the rail and the wall with decorator's caulk, tooled smooth. Paint the rail with gloss or eggshell to match the cornice and skirting board finish.

Picture hooks (also called picture rail hooks or S-hooks) loop over the hooked upper profile of the rail. A length of picture wire or cord hangs from the hook; the picture hangs from the lower end of the wire. A single hook carries up to about 10 kg for most rails; for larger pieces, use two hooks spaced along the wire. The hooks slide along the rail so pictures can be repositioned without tools.

attrs.symptom: Rail pulls away from the wall when loaded
attrs.cause: Rail fixed only to soft plaster with adhesive, without mechanical fixings into the structure behind.
attrs.fix: Refix with screws (or brads) into the masonry or timber grounds behind the plaster. The adhesive alone cannot carry repeated dynamic load from hanging pictures.
